worth_it_explanation: Exclusions decide where the budget does not go, so a missing exclusion spends money on traffic the account already knows is not worth paying for. A converter or existing customer left in targeting keeps drawing paid clicks the brand would have earned for free, and a placement list with no brand-unsafe sites lets ads run next to content the brand would never approve. Each check reads from settings the practitioner can confirm inside the campaign without waiting on data, and the fix is a list edit applied at the account or campaign level, not a rebuild. The savings and the brand protection start the day the exclusion is in place.
intro: Audience and placement exclusions tell Google Ads where not to spend. They keep ads off audiences that should be reached another way, such as people who already converted, and off inventory the brand does not want to appear on, such as low-quality or off-topic placements. These checks confirm that the account excludes converters and existing customers where that fits the goal, that Display and Performance Max campaigns carry placement exclusions, and that a brand-safety exclusion list is in place rather than left empty.

audit_checks.title: Existing customers and recent converters are not excluded from prospecting campaigns
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Prospecting campaigns that do not exclude converters keep paying to reach people who already bought, which inflates the prospecting cost per acquisition and credits new-customer budget with repeat activity. The spend is not always wasted since some advertisers do want to re-reach buyers, so the impact depends on whether the campaign's goal is genuinely new customers.

educate.base:
A prospecting campaign is built to find new customers, so the people who already converted or who are already on the customer list are not the audience it is meant to pay for. Google Ads lets a campaign exclude a data segment, such as past converters or an uploaded Customer Match list, so that ads stop serving to those people in that campaign.

When that exclusion is absent, the prospecting campaign keeps bidding on existing customers and recent buyers. Some of those clicks would have come back to the brand on their own, so the campaign pays for activity it did not need to buy, and the cost per acquisition (CPA) it reports for new customers is mixed with repeat business. Smart Bidding then optimizes toward a target that includes those easy reconversions, which can pull the bidding away from true new-customer value.

Whether this is a problem depends on the goal. A campaign aimed at new-customer acquisition usually benefits from excluding converters and the customer list. A campaign meant to win back lapsed buyers does not since those people are the point. The audit question is whether the exclusion matches the stated goal of the campaign, not whether an exclusion exists everywhere.

A few signs suggest a prospecting campaign is missing the exclusion it should have:
  • The campaign's stated goal is new customers, yet no converter or customer-list exclusion appears in its audience settings
  • The account runs a Customer Match list for targeting but never applies it as an exclusion on acquisition campaigns
  • New-customer acquisition is set as a goal in the campaign, but the same buyers keep appearing in the conversion path
You may want to confirm the campaign goal first, then check whether the exclusion in place lines up with that goal.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, recent purchasers and the email list of past buyers are the obvious exclusion candidates for a new-customer campaign. You may want to confirm that prospecting campaigns exclude a recent-converter segment or the uploaded customer list, and that any new-customer acquisition goal in the campaign is paired with that exclusion rather than left to count repeat purchases.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, the people who already submitted a form or became a client are the ones a prospecting campaign should usually exclude. You may want to confirm that campaigns aimed at new leads exclude the converted-lead segment or the customer list, so the budget reaches fresh prospects rather than re-serving people already in the pipeline.

investigate.intro: Confirm the goal of each prospecting campaign, then check whether its audience settings exclude converters or the customer list to match that goal.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open a prospecting campaign and note its stated goal, for example new-customer acquisition.
Open the campaign's 'Audiences' settings and read the 'Exclusions' section. Note whether a converter segment, a data segment of past buyers, or a Customer Match list is applied as an exclusion.
Repeat for each campaign meant to find new customers. Record any acquisition campaign that carries no relevant exclusion as a candidate finding.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per prospecting campaign, the stated goal and whether a converter or customer-list exclusion is applied. A new-customer campaign with a matching exclusion passes. A new-customer campaign with no converter or customer-list exclusion is a fail recorded with the campaign name. A campaign whose goal is to re-reach buyers is marked not applicable.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the goal and the exclusion status for each prospecting campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that prospecting campaigns on {{site_name}} aimed at new customers do not exclude existing customers or recent converters. Those campaigns keep paying to reach people who already bought, which raises the reported cost per acquisition for new customers and mixes repeat business into the acquisition numbers Smart Bidding learns from. {{voice_recommend}} excluding a recent-converter segment or the customer list from each campaign whose goal is genuinely new customers, while leaving win-back campaigns untouched. After the change, confirm the exclusion appears in each campaign's audience settings and watch whether the new-customer cost per acquisition settles.

audit_checks.title: Display and Performance Max campaigns have no placement exclusions applied
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Display and Performance Max reach a wide pool of sites, apps, and videos by default, so with no placement exclusions a share of spend lands on off-topic or low-quality inventory that rarely converts. The waste accumulates quietly across many small placements rather than in one obvious line, which keeps it from being caught in routine performance reviews.

educate.base:
Display campaigns and Performance Max (PMax) campaigns serve ads across a broad mix of websites, mobile apps, and videos. By default they reach inventory wherever Google's systems expect a result, which means ads can appear on placements the advertiser never reviewed. Placement exclusions are the control that removes specific sites, apps, channels, or whole content categories from that pool.

With no exclusions applied, a portion of the budget flows to placements that do not match the offer, such as mobile games where taps are often accidental, parked domains, or content unrelated to the product. These placements can register clicks and impressions while producing few conversions, so they drain spend without contributing to results.

The control differs by campaign type. Display campaigns accept placement exclusions directly at the campaign or account level. Performance Max accepts account-level placement exclusions and account-level brand-safety settings rather than per-campaign placement lists, so the exclusion work for PMax happens mostly at the account level. A practitioner checking PMax looks for those account-level lists rather than a campaign-level field.

You may want to start from the placements report to see where spend actually went, then decide which sites, apps, or categories to exclude. Excluding inventory the account has no reason to appear on protects both the budget and where the brand shows up.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, mobile-app inventory and arcade-style game placements often draw accidental taps that spend the Display or Performance Max budget without producing sales. You may want to review the placements report for app and game traffic, then exclude the categories or specific apps that show clicks with no purchases.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, broad Display and Performance Max reach can place ads on content far from the buyer's intent, so form submissions stay flat while impressions climb. You may want to review where the spend landed and exclude off-topic sites and app categories that bring traffic but no qualified leads.

investigate.intro: Check whether Display campaigns carry placement exclusions and whether the account has account-level placement exclusions that cover Performance Max.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open a Display campaign, go to 'Content', then 'Placements', and open the 'Exclusions' tab. Note whether any sites, apps, channels, or categories are excluded.
Open the placements or 'Where ads showed' report for the Display and Performance Max campaigns and scan for app or off-topic spend with few or no conversions.

Open account-level settings and check for account-level placement exclusions and brand-safety settings, which are where Performance Max exclusions are applied. Record any campaign or account with no exclusions in place as a candidate finding.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per Display campaign, whether placement exclusions are applied, and for the account, whether account-level placement exclusions cover Performance Max. A Display campaign with exclusions in place and an account with account-level exclusions covering PMax pass. A Display campaign or PMax-running account with no exclusions, paired with off-topic or app spend in the placements report, is a fail recorded with the campaign or account scope.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the placement-exclusion status for each Display campaign and for the account-level Performance Max settings above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that Display and Performance Max campaigns on {{site_name}} run with no placement exclusions applied. Both reach a wide pool of sites, apps, and videos by default, so a share of the budget lands on off-topic or low-quality inventory that brings clicks but few conversions. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the placements report to see where spend went, then excluding the sites, apps, and content categories that do not fit the offer, applying campaign-level exclusions on Display and account-level exclusions for Performance Max. After the change, recheck the placements report to confirm the excluded inventory has dropped out.

audit_checks.title: No brand-safety placement or content exclusion list is in place across campaigns
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When no brand-safety exclusions are set, ads can appear next to content the brand would not approve, which is a reputational exposure rather than only a spend question. A single screenshot of an ad beside objectionable content can do damage that outlasts any short-term efficiency gain, which is why the missing control rates higher than a routine spend leak.

educate.base:
Brand-safety exclusions keep ads off content a brand does not want to be associated with, separate from the performance-based placement exclusions that target weak inventory. Google Ads offers two layers for this. Content-suitability settings, including inventory types and digital content labels on Display and Video campaigns, exclude whole categories of sensitive content. Placement and topic exclusion lists, applied at the account or campaign level, block specific sites, channels, apps, or subjects.

With neither layer in place, ads can run beside content that clashes with the brand, such as violent, sexually suggestive, or politically charged material that still falls within Google's general policies. Google's automatic protections remove the most extreme content, yet a wide band of material the advertiser would reject remains eligible until an exclusion list or a stricter inventory type is set.

The exposure is reputational. A buyer or a client who sees the brand's ad next to objectionable content forms an impression that no efficiency metric offsets. For regulated industries and family brands the bar is higher still because their audiences and partners expect tighter control over adjacency.

You may want to confirm two things rather than one. First, whether content-suitability settings are set to an inventory type stricter than the broadest default on Display and Video. Second, whether an account-level placement or topic exclusion list exists and is actually applied to the live campaigns. A list that exists but is attached to nothing does not protect anything.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, a single screenshot of the brand's ad beside objectionable content can spread among shoppers and damage trust in the storefront. You may want to confirm that Display and Video campaigns use a stricter inventory type than the broadest default and that an account-level exclusion list of unsuitable sites and topics is applied to the running campaigns.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, adjacency to off-brand content can undercut the credibility the offer depends on, especially for professional or regulated services. You may want to confirm content-suitability settings are tightened and that a brand-safety exclusion list is attached to the live campaigns rather than saved and left unused.

learn_more.source_title: About content exclusions for Video campaigns

investigate.intro: Check the content-suitability settings on Display and Video campaigns and confirm whether a brand-safety placement or topic exclusion list exists and is applied to live campaigns.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the account-level content-suitability settings and note the inventory type set for Display and Video, for example whether it sits at the broadest default or a stricter level.
Open the account-level exclusion lists and note whether a placement or topic exclusion list of brand-unsafe sites and subjects exists.

For each live Display, Video, and Performance Max campaign, confirm whether that exclusion list and the suitability settings actually apply to it. Record any campaign with no brand-safety exclusion in effect as a candidate finding.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the account's content-suitability inventory type and whether a brand-safety exclusion list exists and is applied to live campaigns. A stricter-than-default inventory type plus an applied exclusion list passes. A broadest-default inventory type with no applied brand-safety exclusion list is a fail recorded at the account scope, with any campaign left unprotected named alongside it.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the content-suitability setting and the brand-safety exclusion-list status above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs ad campaigns with no brand-safety placement or content exclusion list in effect. Google's automatic protections remove the most extreme content, but a wide band of material the brand would reject stays eligible until a stricter inventory type or an applied exclusion list is set, which leaves the brand exposed to appearing beside content it would not approve. {{voice_recommend}} tightening the content-suitability inventory type on Display and Video, building an account-level exclusion list of unsuitable sites and topics, and confirming that list is attached to every live campaign. After the change, review where ads showed to confirm the excluded content no longer appears.
